In our view therefore the only issue before us is whether the appellant was one of the persons who murdered by Churri Mst Safia Bibi whilst she wrts it't her home on 15.09.2009. 15. A{ter our reassessment of the evidence we are of the view tltat rhc prosecution has not been able to prove beyond a reasonable tloubt that the appellant murdered Mst Safia Bibi by Churri whilst she was in her ltoure t,tt 15.09.2009 for the following reasons; (a) Admittedly this was an unwitnessed incident. The. main plank r''f tht' prosecution case rests on the judicial confession of co-accusccl Atlr.rar.r who is now deceased. Admittedly we can convict the appellant rrn tht' conJession of Adnan if we find it to be voluntary, truthful ancl in lint' with the prosecution case and the confc.ssion has bcctt nratlc itr accordance with law and is corroborated by somc' u n itl pttrcha Lrle evidence from an independent source. In this respect reliance is plact'tl on Azeem Khan V Muhahid Khan (2016 SCMR 27.1) ancl Bahadur V State (PLD 1996 SC 336) (b) Turning to the juclicial confession; (i) The jutlicial confession has been made by one co-accusetl against another and as such this in and of itself crr'.rtt's sonrt' doubt as to its reliabilitv in the absence of anv corroborati\,(' evidence. (ii) It's voluntariness is also in doubt as it rvas nrarlc atrout a month after its maker's arrest during whiclt periotl Ite tr'.tr irr police custody so why woulrl he choose lo mlkt' it trotr' especially as there was hardly any other evider-rte a;iaitrst him ancl he woultl have been aware that it tn.rl lt'.rtl tt,,r potential death sentence when used in eviclence against him as he was so warnecl. (iii) We also have some concerns about the h'uthfulncss oI tho confession as it does not particularly appeal to reasotr that he would wait in the house when tlre accusecl n'ilh his co- ?

Admittedly we can convict the appellant rrn tht' conJession of Adnan if we find it to be voluntary, truthful ancl in lint' with the prosecution case and the confc.ssion has bcctt nratlc itr accordance with law and is corroborated by somc' u n itl pttrcha Lrle evidence from an independent source. In this respect reliance is plact'tl on Azeem Khan V Muhahid Khan (2016 SCMR 27.1) ancl Bahadur V State (PLD 1996 SC 336) (b) Turning to the juclicial confession; (i) The jutlicial confession has been made by one co-accusetl against another and as such this in and of itself crr'.rtt's sonrt' doubt as to its reliabilitv in the absence of anv corroborati\,(' evidence. (ii) It's voluntariness is also in doubt as it rvas nrarlc atrout a month after its maker's arrest during whiclt periotl Ite tr'.tr irr police custody so why woulrl he choose lo mlkt' it trotr' especially as there was hardly any other evider-rte a;iaitrst him ancl he woultl have been aware that it tn.rl lt'.rtl tt,,r potential death sentence when used in eviclence against him as he was so warnecl. (iii) We also have some concerns about the h'uthfulncss oI tho confession as it does not particularly appeal to reasotr that he would wait in the house when tlre accusecl n'ilh his co- ? z- ow accused was tying up the tleceasecl ancl rhe n ntu rtlet'ing h,,'r with a dagger especially as the deceased who hatl no gag itt her mouth would probably have screamed out. -fht'pr('scnc(' of two minor children in the house at the tim!'also nrilitates against his conduct especially as he diel not even knon, n,hr he was going to meet the accusecl at the lrouse. (c) Thus, we have doubts whether the confession <:f Abicl can be sa|:lr relied upon. (d) We also find other corroborative evidence against thc apprcllant trr lrt' lacking and the case of the prosecuti()n to contain ekrubts. For t'r;rnrirle, (D One of the key witnesses to the prosecution case w,l.to coulel have identified the appellant namelv a Mr.A.Khan as purchasing the dagger which was usetl to nrurrlt'r tlrr: deceased was not called before an identilication p"rra(l('in order to identify the accused anrl was not t.ven calleci as a PW without any explanation. No finger prints were takt'n from the dagger and as such there is no evirlenct'to link tht' appellant to the murder weapon i.e the dagger. (ii) Iftikhar his neighbour who the conrplainant allcgedlv callctl to help him enter his house by breaking the lcrks rryas trol called to corroborate this evidencc. Neither the locks, grill or hammer and chisel which was used to break the lor-k were exhibited in eviclence which creates doubt as t() wl]etlrer th(' complainanl actually br(-'ke into his housc as lre lra.l a ke,r. (iii) The wire used to tie his wife was also not exhibiteel into evidence. (ir) From the evidence there app(.ars to lravr, b(,(.n (,nnrit\' between the accusecl anci the complainant ()\'er the pavnrr'r'tt of money and the sale of inheriteei lanel wl.ricl.r provit{r,,1 a motive lor the complainant to fix the appcll.rnt irt a l.tls| tast which is the appellants plea in his 5.342 Statemcnt.rnd evidence under oath. (u) If the accused had enmity with the comPlainant then in our view it does not appeal to logic, con.rn.ron sensr'or r'()asor'l that he would murder the complainant's wife.

No finger prints were takt'n from the dagger and as such there is no evirlenct'to link tht' appellant to the murder weapon i.e the dagger. (ii) Iftikhar his neighbour who the conrplainant allcgedlv callctl to help him enter his house by breaking the lcrks rryas trol called to corroborate this evidencc.
